cover.title: Cloud Security Fundamentals & __Shared Responsibility__
cover.hook: 95% of cloud breaches are caused by the customer, not the provider. Know exactly where your responsibility starts.

slides.label: The Shared Responsibility Model
slides.method: WHAT — Who Secures What in the Cloud
slides.heading: Shared Responsibility: __Your Line in the Sand__

body.text: **The Shared Responsibility Model** defines the exact boundary between what the cloud provider secures and what you secure. The provider owns physical infrastructure, hypervisor, and global network. You own everything on top — OS configurations, application code, data, IAM policies, network settings, and encryption choices.

**The model shifts by service type:** IaaS (EC2, GCE, Azure VM) gives you the most control and the most responsibility — you manage OS, runtime, app, and data. PaaS (App Engine, Elastic Beanstalk) hands the runtime and OS to the provider so you focus on app and data. SaaS (Gmail, Office 365) means the provider manages nearly everything — your job is user access and data governance only.

**Why this matters for your career:** Every cloud breach investigation starts with the same question — whose responsibility was the misconfigured setting? Analysts who can answer that immediately are far more valuable than those who have to look it up.

slides.time: 20 min · aws.amazon.com/compliance/shared-responsibility-model · cloud.google.com/architecture/framework/security

slides.label: Top Cloud Attack Vectors
slides.method: WHAT — How Cloud Environments Get Breached
slides.heading: Cloud Attack Vectors: __How Breaches Actually Happen__

body.text: **Misconfigured S3 buckets set to public read** — the single most common cloud breach cause. Millions of records exposed from companies that clicked the wrong toggle.
**Over-privileged IAM roles with wildcard permissions** — granting `*` on everything when only `s3:GetObject` on one bucket was needed. The blast radius of a stolen credential becomes catastrophic.
**SSRF exploiting the metadata endpoint at 169.254.169.254** — a vulnerable web app on EC2 can be tricked into returning the instance's IAM temporary credentials to an attacker.
**API keys committed to GitHub** — automated bots scan every public commit within seconds. Keys found this way are exploited within minutes of exposure.
**Default credentials never changed after provisioning** — databases, dashboards, and management panels spun up and forgotten with factory-default passwords.

slides.time: 20 min · Study each vector — you will see all five in cloud penetration tests

slides.label: Cloud Security Posture Management
slides.heading: CSPM: __Automated Audit of Your Cloud__

body.text: **Cloud Security Posture Management (CSPM)** is continuous automated auditing of cloud configurations for misconfigurations and compliance violations. Instead of manual spot checks, CSPM tools run constantly and alert the moment a dangerous setting appears.

**Three free open-source CSPM tools every security professional should know:** Prowler (AWS-focused, github.com/prowler-cloud/prowler — over 300 checks aligned to CIS, PCI DSS, and GDPR) · Scout Suite (multi-cloud, github.com/nccgroup/ScoutSuite — generates an HTML report of every misconfiguration across AWS, Azure, and GCP) · CloudSploit (GitHub — lightweight and beginner-friendly).

**The rule:** run a CSPM tool against your own account on Day 1 of any cloud project before writing a single line of code. The findings on a brand new account are always surprising — and always educational.

slides.time: 20 min · github.com/prowler-cloud/prowler — install and run today

slides.label: Choose Your Cloud Platform
slides.method: HOW — Picking Your Primary Cloud Focus
slides.heading: Cloud Platform: __Pick One and Go Deep__

body.text: **AWS (Amazon Web Services):** 32% global market share, most job postings, richest free security lab ecosystem (flaws.cloud, CloudGoat, cloud-specific CTFs). The safest career choice if you are deciding blind.
**GCP (Google Cloud Platform):** Excellent free tier, genuinely the best free training via Cloud Skills Boost (monthly lab credits, no credit card needed), strong in data and ML environments.

**Azure (Microsoft):** Dominant in enterprise environments. SC-200 is one of the most in-demand certifications right now. If your target employers run Microsoft infrastructure — and most large enterprises do — Azure is the choice.

slides.note: **Strategy:** Pick ONE and go deep this phase. Breadth comes after depth. A shallow understanding of three clouds is worth less than expert-level knowledge of one.
slides.time: 15 min · Create a free account on your chosen platform today

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 71 Checklist
steps: **Create** a free account on your chosen cloud platform — AWS free tier / GCP free tier / Azure $200 credit
**Enable MFA** on the root/owner account immediately — before anything else
**Install the CLI** — aws-cli, gcloud, or az — and authenticate successfully from your terminal
**Run Scout Suite or Prowler** against your fresh account — document every finding
**Read** the Shared Responsibility Model page for your chosen provider — note exactly what YOU are responsible for
slides.time: Total Day 71 Study Time: 120 – 150 minutes (account setup day)

**Install** the CLI tool for your platform — authenticate with `aws configure` / `gcloud init` / `az login`
**Run** Scout Suite or Prowler — `pip install scoutsuite && scout aws` — save the HTML report

slides.time: Hands-On Task: 90 – 120 minutes (includes account and CLI setup)

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Cloud security is the single fastest-growing and highest-paying security specialization. Average cloud security engineer salary in the US: $130–160k. Every skill built in the past 70 days — Linux, Python, networking, SIEM — applies directly in cloud environments. You are not starting over. You are adding a critical layer on top of everything you already know.
slides.sub: The three attack vectors worth memorising cold before any cloud interview: misconfigured public S3 buckets, over-privileged IAM roles, and SSRF against the instance metadata service. These three alone account for the majority of real-world cloud breaches documented in public breach disclosures over the past five years. Know each one, know the attacker's exact technique, and know the one configuration change that prevents it.
slides.mistake: Starting cloud labs without enabling billing alerts. Accidental charges from forgotten resources are extremely common — a NAT Gateway left running overnight, an EC2 instance left on for a week, a forgotten Elasticsearch cluster. Enable a billing alert at $5 on AWS and Azure BEFORE running any lab. This is the first thing, not an afterthought.

cover.title: AWS IAM: Users, Roles, Policies & __Least Privilege__
cover.hook: AWS IAM is where 90% of cloud breaches begin. Master it and you prevent most attacks before they start.

slides.label: IAM Core Components
slides.method: WHAT — The Four Building Blocks of AWS IAM
slides.heading: IAM: __The Four Components You Must Know__

body.text: **AWS Identity and Access Management (IAM)** controls who can do what to which AWS resource. Four core components build the entire system: **Users** (long-term credentials for humans) · **Groups** (collections of users sharing a policy) · **Roles** (temporary credentials for services, applications, and cross-account access) · **Policies** (JSON documents defining Allow or Deny for specific actions on specific resources).

**The golden rule of AWS IAM:** never use the root account after initial setup. The root account has unrestricted access to everything in the account and cannot be limited by any policy. Create an admin IAM user immediately after account creation and lock root credentials away permanently. Any SOC analyst who sees root account activity in CloudTrail logs treats it as a critical alert.

**Explicit Deny always beats Allow** — regardless of what other policies say. If a Deny exists anywhere in the policy evaluation chain, the action is blocked. Understanding this evaluation order is the key to debugging unexpected IAM permission errors without guessing.

slides.time: 20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/IAM/latest/UserGuide

slides.label: IAM Policy Structure
slides.method: WHAT — Reading and Writing IAM Policies
slides.heading: Policy Structure: __Read Any AWS Policy__

body.text: **Condition blocks** are the most underused IAM feature. You can restrict actions to specific IP ranges, require MFA, limit to specific time windows, or enforce encryption headers on S3 uploads — all without changing the core Allow. Policies without Conditions are almost always broader than they need to be.

**Resource ARN specificity matters:** `arn:aws:s3:::*` grants access to every bucket in your account. `arn:aws:s3:::my-bucket/*` grants access to only one bucket. The difference between these two is the difference between a contained breach and a total data loss.

slides.time: 25 min · Use IAM Policy Simulator at console.aws.amazon.com/policysim

slides.label: IAM Roles for EC2
slides.method: WHAT — Temporary Credentials vs Long-Lived Keys
slides.heading: IAM Roles: __Never Store Keys on EC2__

body.text: **The single most important IAM security practice:** never store AWS access keys on an EC2 instance as environment variables, config files, or in application code. Assign an IAM Role to the instance instead. The application retrieves temporary, automatically-rotating credentials via the metadata service at runtime — no long-lived secret exists to steal, rotate, or accidentally expose.

# WRONG — hardcoded keys on EC2:
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=AKIA...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=abc123...

# RIGHT — IAM Role attached to EC2 instance:
# Assign the role at launch, then in your application:
import boto3
# Credentials are retrieved automatically from the role
s3 = boto3.client('s3')  # No keys needed anywhere

**IAM Access Analyzer** is a free AWS service that identifies roles, S3 buckets, KMS keys, and Lambda functions shared with external accounts or the internet. Run `aws accessanalyzer list-findings` monthly. Enable it in every region simultaneously — external access findings routinely appear in regions that are assumed to be unused.

slides.time: 20 min · Enable IAM Access Analyzer in your account now

slides.label: Auditing IAM with CLI
slides.heading: IAM Audit: __Find Problems Before Attackers Do__

body.text:
# Generate and retrieve the full IAM credential report:
aws iam generate-credential-report
aws iam get-credential-report --query 'Content' \
  --output text | base64 -d > iam_report.csv

# The CSV contains every IAM user with:
# - Last access dates for console and API
# - MFA enabled status
# - Access key ages and last used dates
# - Password last changed date

# Red flags to immediately investigate:
# Any user: mfa_active = false
# Any access_key: last_used_date > 90 days ago
# Root account: access_key_1_active = true
# Root account: mfa_active = false

**Run this audit monthly at minimum.** Any IAM user with console access and no MFA is a critical finding — the equivalent of leaving an admin account with no password on the public internet. Any access key unused for 90+ days should be deactivated immediately — dormant credentials are prime targets for credential-stuffing attacks.

slides.time: 20 min · Run the credential report against your account right now

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 72 Checklist
steps: **Complete** flaws.cloud Levels 1–4 — each level teaches one real S3 or IAM misconfiguration by letting you exploit it safely
**Run** `aws iam generate-credential-report` — review the output for MFA gaps and stale access keys
**Create** an IAM user with least-privilege policy — only the permissions needed for one specific task
**Enable** IAM Access Analyzer — run `aws accessanalyzer list-findings` and review every result
**Understand** the Explicit Deny evaluation rule — test it with the IAM Policy Simulator
slides.time: Total Day 72 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.heading: Your Hands-On __Task__ for Day 72
steps: **Complete** flaws.cloud Levels 1–4 — for each level: write the misconfiguration, the CLI command that revealed it, the one-line fix
**Run** the IAM credential report — flag every user without MFA and every key unused for 90+ days
**Create** a restrictive IAM policy for S3 read-only on one bucket — test it with the Policy Simulator

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: flaws.cloud was built by Scott Piper, a former Netflix cloud security engineer. Every level represents a misconfiguration found on real client engagements. Completing all six levels and explaining each finding and fix is a genuine interview differentiator — it shows practical hands-on knowledge rather than just certification studying.
slides.sub: The IAM Roles vs Access Keys distinction is the most important security architecture decision in AWS. Long-lived access keys are static credentials — they do not expire, they can be stolen silently, and they appear in breach after breach. IAM Roles issue temporary credentials with automatic rotation via STS. There is almost never a legitimate reason to use access keys on an EC2 instance. In any cloud architecture review, access keys stored on instances are an immediate critical finding.
slides.mistake: Enabling billing alerts after starting labs instead of before. Cloud bills from forgotten resources accumulate silently. A single NAT Gateway left running generates $30–40/month in AWS with zero usage. Set a billing alarm at $1 in CloudWatch before running any lab session. Check the billing console at the end of every session. This discipline saves real money and teaches cost awareness — a skill cloud security engineers are expected to have.

cover.title: AWS S3 Security, VPC & __Security Groups__
cover.hook: Misconfigured S3 buckets and open security groups have leaked billions of records. Here's how to get both right permanently.

slides.label: S3 Bucket Security Essentials
slides.method: WHAT — Securing S3 from Day One
slides.heading: S3 Security: __Four Settings That Prevent Breaches__

body.text: **S3 buckets are private by default.** Public access is a setting you must explicitly enable — and almost never should. The 'Block Public Access' setting has four independent toggles that must ALL be switched on for complete protection. A single unchecked toggle is the misconfiguration responsible for multiple billion-record data breaches in the past five years.

# Check a bucket's public access block settings via CLI:
aws s3api get-public-access-block --bucket BUCKETNAME

# Expected output (all must be true):
# BlockPublicAcls: true
# IgnorePublicAcls: true
# BlockPublicPolicy: true
# RestrictPublicBuckets: true

# Audit bucket ACL permissions:
aws s3api get-bucket-acl --bucket BUCKETNAME

# Enable block public access if not set:
aws s3api put-public-access-block \
  --bucket BUCKETNAME \
  --public-access-block-configuration \
  BlockPublicAcls=true,IgnorePublicAcls=true,\
BlockPublicPolicy=true,RestrictPublicBuckets=true

**Enable versioning and MFA Delete on critical buckets.** Versioning keeps copies of every object version. MFA Delete requires a physical MFA token to permanently delete any version — this single control prevents ransomware from destroying your S3 data even if full AWS credentials are compromised.

slides.time: 20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/AmazonS3/latest/userguide/security-best-practices.html

slides.label: VPC Architecture Best Practice
slides.heading: VPC Architecture: __Keep Your Workloads Off the Internet__

body.text: **Public subnets for load balancers only** — the only tier that ever needs a public IP. No application server, database, or internal service should ever be here directly.
**Private subnets for EC2, RDS, Lambda** — all compute and data resources live here with no direct internet route. The only path in is through the load balancer.
**NAT Gateway for outbound-only internet** — private subnet resources can initiate outbound connections (for software updates, API calls) but inbound connections from the internet are impossible.
**No Internet Gateway attached to private subnet route tables** — the most common VPC misconfiguration. A single accidental route entry makes a 'private' subnet fully public.

This architecture pattern alone prevents the majority of direct attacks against application and database tiers. A misconfigured EC2 database with a public IP in a public subnet is a direct attack surface — this pattern makes that impossible by design.

slides.time: 20 min · Draw this architecture on paper before building it in AWS

slides.label: Security Groups
slides.method: WHAT — Stateful Firewall Rules for Every Resource
slides.heading: Security Groups: __Your Last Line of Host Defence__

body.text: **Security groups** are stateful virtual firewalls attached to EC2 instances and other AWS resources. Rules define allowed traffic by protocol, port range, and source or destination. Stateful means return traffic is automatically allowed — you only define inbound rules for accepted connections.

# Audit every security group open to the internet:
aws ec2 describe-security-groups \
  --filters "Name=ip-permission.cidr,Values=0.0.0.0/0" \
  --query 'SecurityGroups[*].{ID:GroupId,Name:GroupName,\
Rules:IpPermissions}'

# Critical rules — NEVER allow from 0.0.0.0/0:
# Port 22  (SSH)
# Port 3389 (RDP)
# Port 3306 (MySQL)
# Port 5432 (PostgreSQL)

# Correct approach — restrict SSH to your IP only:
# Source: 203.0.113.45/32   (your static IP)
# Or: reference another security group ID instead of CIDR
# Security group references auto-update when instances change

**Use security group references instead of CIDR ranges** wherever possible. Referencing another security group ID means the rule automatically applies to all instances in that group — regardless of their IP addresses. This scales perfectly and never requires manual updates when instances are replaced.

slides.time: 25 min · Run the CLI audit and fix every 0.0.0.0/0 finding

slides.label: S3 Encryption Standards
slides.method: HOW — Encryption Options and When to Use Each
slides.heading: S3 Encryption: __AES-256 is the Floor, Not the Ceiling__

body.text: **Server-side encryption is now the default on all new S3 buckets** — SSE-S3 with AES-256 is applied automatically. This is the minimum acceptable baseline for all data. For regulated data (healthcare, financial, government), the requirements go further.

**SSE-KMS** uses your own KMS Customer Managed Key and logs every single decryption event via CloudTrail. This creates a complete audit trail of every access to sensitive data — required for HIPAA, PCI DSS, and SOC 2 compliance. You can revoke the key and immediately make encrypted data unreadable, even to AWS itself.

**SSE-C** lets you supply your own encryption keys entirely. AWS never stores your key — you must send it with every request. Maximum control, maximum operational burden. Used only when regulatory requirements specifically demand customer-controlled keys with zero cloud provider access.

slides.note: **CloudMapper** (github.com/duo-labs/cloudmapper) generates a visual diagram of your entire VPC topology and attack paths. Run it after building your VPC to immediately see any unintended exposure paths.
slides.time: 15 min · Verify encryption is enabled on every bucket in your account

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 73 Checklist
steps: **Create** an S3 bucket — upload a test file — verify it is NOT publicly accessible via browser URL
**Create** a VPC with one public subnet and one private subnet — draw the diagram before building
**Launch** an EC2 t2.micro in the private subnet — confirm it has no public IP
**Create** a security group allowing SSH only from your specific IP address — not 0.0.0.0/0
**Run** the CLI command to audit all security groups for open 0.0.0.0/0 rules — document every finding
slides.time: Total Day 73 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: When designing a VPC, draw the architecture on paper first — public subnets, private subnets, NAT Gateways, route tables, Internet Gateway, and security group boundaries. Engineers who can draw their cloud infrastructure from memory in interviews consistently outperform those who only know the console clicks. Architecture fluency is a seniority signal.
slides.sub: The security group audit command — `aws ec2 describe-security-groups --filters Name=ip-permission.cidr,Values=0.0.0.0/0` — is one of the first commands cloud security engineers run on a new engagement. Wide-open security groups are the second most common finding in AWS penetration tests after IAM over-privilege. Running this in your own account and fixing every result is the exact work a cloud security engineer does on Day 1 of any assessment.
slides.mistake: Leaving NAT Gateways running after finishing a lab. NAT Gateways cost approximately $0.045 per hour even when completely idle with zero traffic passing through them. That is $32/month for a gateway doing nothing. Always delete NAT Gateways at the end of every lab session. They are the single most common source of unexpected AWS bills among security learners.

cover.title: AWS CloudTrail, GuardDuty & __Security Hub__
cover.hook: AWS gives you powerful native threat detection tools. Most people never turn them on. Today you do.

slides.label: AWS CloudTrail
slides.method: WHAT — Your Forensic Audit Trail for Every API Call
slides.heading: CloudTrail: __Every AWS Action, Recorded Forever__

body.text: **AWS CloudTrail** records every single AWS API call made in your account — who called it, from which IP address, at what time, with what parameters, and whether it succeeded or failed. It is the forensic audit trail for every action taken in your cloud environment. In any AWS breach investigation, CloudTrail is always the first data source every analyst examines.

**Configuration that matters:** enable CloudTrail in all regions simultaneously — not just your primary region. Attacks frequently originate from regions that appear unused. Store logs in an S3 bucket with log file integrity validation turned on — this detects any tampering with audit logs before the evidence reaches investigators.

# Essential CloudTrail queries via CloudWatch Logs Insights:

# Who deleted a security group:
filter EventName = 'DeleteSecurityGroup'

# All IAM changes (critical — any IAM modification is suspicious):
filter EventSource = 'iam.amazonaws.com'

# Root account usage (should always be zero):
filter userIdentity.type = 'Root'

# Failed console logins — brute force detection:
filter EventName = 'ConsoleLogin'
  AND errorMessage = 'Failed authentication'

slides.time: 20 min · docs.aws.amazon.com/awscloudtrail/latest/userguide

slides.label: AWS GuardDuty
slides.method: WHAT — Automated Threat Detection Without Rules
slides.heading: GuardDuty: __ML Threat Detection Running 24/7__

body.text: **What it analyses automatically:** CloudTrail management events, VPC Flow Logs, and DNS query logs — no agent installation, no configuration required, no log shipping setup.
**Cryptocurrency mining detection** — identifies EC2 instances communicating with known mining pools. Often the first sign of a compromised instance being monetised.
**Credential exfiltration** — detects API calls from known malicious IP addresses, TOR exit nodes, and impossible-travel scenarios (login from Pakistan and Singapore within 30 minutes).
**C2 communication** — detects outbound connections to known command-and-control infrastructure from your instances.
**Anomalous API call patterns** — machine learning baseline of normal API usage detects sudden spikes or unusual API calls from compromised credentials.

**Free 30-day trial. Approximately $4/month after for small accounts.** Enable it — it catches entire categories of attacks that signature-based tools miss entirely. Never running GuardDuty is the cloud equivalent of running a server with no IDS.

slides.time: 20 min · aws.amazon.com/guardduty — enable the free trial right now

slides.label: AWS Security Hub
slides.method: WHAT — Your Single Pane of Glass for AWS Security
slides.heading: Security Hub: __One Score for Your Entire Posture__

body.text: **AWS Security Hub** is the central aggregator for findings from GuardDuty, Inspector, Macie, IAM Access Analyzer, and third-party integrations. Instead of checking six separate dashboards, Security Hub pulls everything into a single view with a unified security score from 0 to 100%.

**CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark** checks run automatically inside Security Hub — over 170 automated compliance controls evaluating your account configuration continuously. Each failed check includes a specific severity rating, affected resource, and AWS-authored remediation guidance. The score on a fresh account is almost never above 50%.

**MITRE ATT&CK mapping** is built into GuardDuty findings — every detected threat is tagged with the relevant ATT&CK technique. This is the same framework you studied in the detection engineering phase, now applied directly to cloud attack patterns. The concepts transfer exactly.

slides.time: 20 min · aws.amazon.com/security-hub — enable alongside GuardDuty

slides.label: The Minimum Viable AWS Security Stack
slides.method: HOW — The Trio Every AWS Account Needs
slides.heading: AWS Security Trio: __Blind Without All Three__

body.text: **CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Security Hub** is the minimum viable AWS security monitoring stack. Any AWS environment running without all three is flying completely blind — no audit trail, no threat detection, no compliance visibility.

**The interview answer to 'how would you set up security monitoring in a new AWS account?'** starts here. Enable CloudTrail in all regions with S3 storage and integrity validation. Enable GuardDuty with the free trial and all optional data sources. Enable Security Hub with CIS Benchmark checks. This trio takes under 20 minutes to configure and covers the entire basic detection stack.

**CloudTrail data events** — S3 object-level logging that records every GetObject and PutObject — are not included in the default trail. They cost extra and generate enormous volumes on busy accounts. Enable them only for your highest-value buckets, not all buckets globally, to prevent surprise billing spikes.

slides.note: **Cost reality:** GuardDuty 30-day free trial, then ~$4/month. Security Hub 30-day free trial, then ~$1/month for small accounts. CloudTrail first trail free. The entire stack costs less than most security tools charge per hour.
slides.time: 25 min · Enable all three in your AWS account before moving on

slides.label: What to Learn Today
slides.heading: Day 74 Checklist
steps: **Enable CloudTrail** in all regions — log to S3 with integrity validation turned on
**Enable GuardDuty** free trial — explore the finding categories in the console
**Enable Security Hub** with CIS Benchmark checks — note your initial security score
**Query CloudTrail** for your own recent API calls — find your console logins and S3 actions
**Document** every Security Hub FAILED check and the specific remediation action for each
slides.time: Total Day 74 Study Time: 90 – 120 minutes

slides.sub: GuardDuty finding severity levels map directly to response priority: HIGH (7.0–8.9) requires immediate investigation — escalate immediately. MEDIUM (4.0–6.9) requires investigation within 24 hours. LOW (1.0–3.9) can be reviewed weekly. Understanding this triage framework and being able to articulate it in an interview demonstrates genuine operational thinking rather than tool knowledge alone.
slides.mistake: Enabling CloudTrail data events for all S3 buckets globally. S3 data events log every GetObject call — in a busy account with thousands of objects being accessed daily, this generates millions of log entries per day. The cost scales with volume and can generate $50–200/month on active accounts. Enable data events selectively on your highest-value buckets only: the ones containing PII, credentials, or business-critical data.

cover.title: GCP Security: IAM, Cloud Logging & __Security Command Center__
cover.hook: Google Cloud powers YouTube, Gmail, and thousands of enterprise workloads. Here's the complete GCP security model.

slides.label: GCP IAM Fundamentals
slides.heading: GCP IAM: __Hierarchy Makes It Powerful and Dangerous__

body.text: **GCP IAM** binds three things together: a **Principal** (who) + a **Role** (what permissions) + a **Resource** (which GCP resource the role applies to). Three role types: Basic (Owner/Editor/Viewer — overly broad, avoid in production), Predefined (service-specific, hundreds available), and Custom (you define exactly which permissions are included).

**The critical difference from AWS:** GCP IAM is hierarchical. Policies applied at the Organisation level inherit down through every Folder, then every Project, then every Resource. A single misconfigured org-level binding grants access across every project in the entire organisation — potentially thousands of resources from one mistake.

**Service Accounts** are the GCP equivalent of AWS IAM Roles for workloads. Create one dedicated service account per application with only the permissions it needs. Avoid service account JSON key files — they are long-lived credentials that routinely get committed to source control. Use Workload Identity Federation for keyless authentication from outside GCP.

slides.time: 20 min · cloud.google.com/iam/docs

slides.label: Cloud Logging & Audit Logs
slides.heading: Cloud Logging: __Your GCP Forensic Starting Point__

body.text:
# Admin Activity logs are always on and always free:
# Records all admin API calls, IAM changes, config modifications

# Find all recent IAM policy changes:
gcloud logging read \
  'protoPayload.methodName="SetIamPolicy"' \
  --limit=20

# Find all service account key creations (critical alert):
gcloud logging read \
  'protoPayload.methodName="google.iam.admin.v1.\
CreateServiceAccountKey"' --limit=10

# Find all resource deletions in last hour:
gcloud logging read \
  'protoPayload.methodName:"delete" \
  timestamp>"2026-04-23T00:00:00Z"' --limit=20

**Data Access logs must be enabled manually** — they are not on by default. Enable them for Cloud Storage, BigQuery, and any service holding sensitive data. These record every read and write to your data. Without them enabled, an attacker can exfiltrate your entire Cloud Storage bucket and leave no forensic trace.

slides.time: 20 min · Enable Data Access logs for your critical GCP services

slides.label: Security Command Center
slides.method: WHAT — GCP's Native Security Posture Platform
slides.heading: Security Command Center: __Free CSPM Built into GCP__

body.text: **Security Command Center (SCC)** is GCP's native CSPM and threat detection platform. The Standard tier is completely free and available to every GCP project. No installation, no agent, no configuration — enable it and findings appear immediately.

**What SCC Standard detects out of the box:** publicly accessible Cloud Storage buckets · overly permissive firewall rules allowing all ingress · over-privileged service accounts with Editor or Owner roles · cryptomining VM activity via anomalous CPU usage patterns · anomalous API call volumes from service accounts.

**GCP Firewall Rules** operate at the VPC network level — unlike AWS security groups which attach to individual instances. Best practice: create an explicit deny-all-ingress default rule for every network, then add specific allow rules using network tags. Target-tag-based rules apply to logical groups of instances automatically and scale without manual updates.

slides.time: 20 min · Enable Security Command Center Standard in your GCP project

slides.label: GCP IAM Recommender
slides.method: HOW — Machine Learning Enforces Least Privilege
slides.heading: IAM Recommender: __ML Does the Cleanup Work__

body.text: **GCP's IAM Recommender** uses machine learning to analyse actual permission usage over 90 days and automatically recommend removing permissions that were never used. In production environments, following Recommender's suggestions typically reduces the blast radius of a compromised account by 60–80%.

**How to access it:** GCP Console → IAM & Admin → Recommendations. Every overly-broad binding gets a specific recommendation: 'Replace role roles/editor on project X with roles/storage.objectViewer on bucket Y — this binding has 847 permissions but only 2 were used in the past 90 days.'

**This is what cloud security engineers do in enterprise environments.** IAM Recommender does the discovery work automatically. Your job is reviewing the recommendations, approving sensible ones, and documenting the rationale for any you reject. Enable it in every GCP project you manage.

slides.sub: GCP and AWS IAM follow the same underlying principle — least privilege — but the implementation differs in one critical way. AWS evaluates policies per request with explicit Deny overriding Allow. GCP uses policy inheritance down a resource hierarchy, meaning a single org-level binding can grant access to thousands of resources simultaneously. Understanding this hierarchy difference — and being able to articulate it clearly in an interview — demonstrates that you understand both platforms conceptually rather than just knowing the console clicks.
slides.mistake: Creating service account JSON key files and storing them locally or in environment variables. Service account JSON keys are long-lived credentials equivalent to a username and password — they do not expire unless manually rotated. If one is committed to any public repository, assume it was found and exploited within 60 seconds. Always use Workload Identity Federation or metadata-based authentication instead. If you must create a key, set an expiry and rotate it monthly.

cover.title: Azure Security: Entra ID, Defender for Cloud & __Sentinel__
cover.hook: Azure dominates enterprise. Microsoft Sentinel is the world's fastest-growing cloud SIEM. Here's the full security stack.

slides.label: Microsoft Entra ID
slides.method: WHAT — The Identity Layer Behind Everything Azure
slides.heading: Entra ID: __Compromise This and Everything Falls__

body.text: **Microsoft Entra ID** (formerly Azure Active Directory) is the cloud identity platform behind every Azure deployment. It manages users, groups, app registrations, managed identities, and conditional access policies. Entra ID is the authentication layer for the entire Microsoft ecosystem — Azure, Microsoft 365, Teams, SharePoint, and every third-party SaaS that uses 'Sign in with Microsoft'.

**Three non-negotiable Entra ID security controls:** MFA enforcement for all users (never optional for admin accounts) · Conditional Access policies that require MFA from untrusted locations and block legacy authentication protocols · Privileged Identity Management (PIM) for just-in-time admin access — admins only have elevated rights for the duration of an approved task, not permanently.

**Azure RBAC** scopes at four levels: Management Group → Subscription → Resource Group → individual Resource. Check all current assignments with `az role assignment list --all --output table`. Any Owner-role assignment should be treated as a critical finding requiring review — Owner grants full control including access management.

slides.time: 20 min · learn.microsoft.com/azure

slides.label: Microsoft Defender for Cloud
slides.heading: Defender for Cloud: __Your Azure Security Score__

body.text: **Secure Score (0–100%)** grades your Azure security posture with recommendations ranked by security impact. The initial score on a fresh account is always educational — and almost always below 50%.
**CSPM at no additional cost** — the free tier covers core posture management across your Azure environment with actionable recommendations and compliance mapping.
**Multi-cloud coverage** — Defender for Cloud extends to AWS and GCP through cloud connectors. One pane of glass for your security posture across all three major cloud providers simultaneously.
**Workload Protection Plans** (paid) add runtime threat detection for VMs, containers, databases, Key Vault, and App Service — similar to GuardDuty in AWS but per-resource-type licensing.

**Start with the Secure Score.** Every recommendation includes: the specific misconfiguration, the affected resources, the risk it creates, and the exact remediation steps. Work through recommendations from highest impact to lowest — the score goes up visibly with each fix.

slides.time: 20 min · Open Defender for Cloud in your Azure account right now

slides.label: Microsoft Sentinel & KQL
slides.heading: Sentinel: __The Fastest-Growing Enterprise SIEM__

body.text: **Microsoft Sentinel** is a cloud-native SIEM and SOAR built on Azure Log Analytics. Over 200 free data connectors bring in Microsoft 365, Azure services, and third-party tools. Pre-built analytics rules, MITRE ATT&CK coverage map, threat hunting workbooks, and automated response playbooks via Logic Apps — all in one platform.

# KQL Queries for Security Analysts in Sentinel:

# Failed login leaderboard — brute force detection:
SecurityEvent
| where EventID == 4625
| summarize FailedLogins=count() by Account, IpAddress
| sort by FailedLogins desc

# All destructive actions — deletion monitoring:
AzureActivity
| where OperationName contains "delete"
| project TimeGenerated, Caller, OperationName

# New Owner role assignments — privilege escalation:
AuditLogs
| where OperationName == "Add member to role"
| where TargetResources[0].modifiedProperties contains "Owner"

**KQL reads almost like English** once you know the core verbs: `where` (filter), `project` (select columns), `summarize` (aggregate), `sort` (order), `join` (combine tables), `extend` (add computed columns). Coming from Kibana KQL, the transition takes days — not weeks.

cover.title: Zero Trust Architecture & __Secrets Management__
cover.hook: Perimeter security is dead. Zero Trust assumes every connection is hostile until proven otherwise. Here's how to build it in the cloud.

slides.label: Zero Trust Core Principles
slides.heading: Zero Trust: __Three Principles, Every Control Maps Here__

body.text: **NIST SP 800-207** defines Zero Trust with three core principles that every modern cloud security control maps to: **Verify explicitly** — always authenticate and authorise based on all available signals (identity, location, device health, service, workload) — never trust based on network location alone. Being inside the VPC means nothing.

**Use least privilege access** — limit access with just-in-time and just-enough-access. Minimise scope, duration, and permissions for every identity, human or machine. An account that only needs read access to one S3 bucket should have exactly that — not S3 full access, not account-level permissions.

**Assume breach** — design as if attackers are already inside the network. Minimise blast radius through segmentation. Encrypt all data in transit and at rest. Use analytics and logging to detect anomalies. Every security control you have built in this 90-day series maps to one of these three principles.

slides.time: 20 min · nvlpubs.nist.gov/nistpubs/SpecialPublications/NIST.SP.800-207.pdf — free PDF

slides.label: Secrets Management
slides.method: WHAT — The Right Way to Handle Credentials
slides.heading: Secrets Management: __No Credential Left in Code__

body.text: **AWS Secrets Manager** — stores database passwords, API keys, and OAuth tokens with automatic rotation for RDS databases. Applications retrieve secrets at runtime via IAM role — no human ever sees a production password in plaintext.
**GCP Secret Manager** — version-controlled secrets with IAM-based access control. Every access is logged. Secret versions can be disabled or destroyed independently. Ideal for GCP workloads.
**Azure Key Vault** — secrets, certificates, and cryptographic keys in a tamper-proof HSM-backed store. Managed identities retrieve secrets without any credentials in code.
**HashiCorp Vault** — open-source, multi-cloud, self-hosted secret management. More operational complexity than cloud-native options but works across all providers and on-premises environments.

**The golden rule:** any credential that appears in source code — even briefly, even in a private repo — must be treated as compromised immediately. Automated secret-scanning bots monitor GitHub continuously. Keys found in commits are typically exploited within minutes of exposure.

slides.time: 20 min · Store your first secret in your cloud's native secret manager today

slides.label: Defense in Depth in Cloud
slides.heading: Defence in Depth: __Seven Layers, Each Independently Defensive__

body.text: **Defence in depth** means no single control is your last line of defence. If any layer fails or is bypassed, the next layer provides independent protection. In cloud architecture, this looks like a deliberate stack of controls from the internet edge to the database.

Layer 1: WAF → filter malicious HTTP requests at the edge
Layer 2: Load Balancer → no direct server exposure, TLS termination
Layer 3: Application in private subnet → zero public IP, no direct route
Layer 4: Database → no internet route, encrypted at rest (AES-256)
Layer 5: Encryption in transit → TLS 1.2+ on every connection everywhere
Layer 6: SIEM detection → CloudTrail + GuardDuty + Security Hub alerting
Layer 7: Incident response runbook → tested and rehearsed, not theoretical

# An attacker who bypasses Layer 1 still faces Layers 2-7.
# No single misconfiguration creates a complete breach path.

slides.time: 20 min · Draw your seven-layer defence architecture diagram

slides.label: Web Application Firewall
slides.method: HOW — Filtering Attacks Before They Reach Your App
slides.heading: WAF: __OWASP Top 10 Protection at the Edge__

body.text: **Web Application Firewalls** filter HTTP/S traffic at the network edge before it reaches your application servers. Every major cloud provider offers a managed WAF: AWS WAF · GCP Cloud Armor · Azure WAF on Application Gateway. Each has managed rule groups providing turnkey OWASP Top 10 protection with no custom rule writing required.

**What a WAF blocks automatically with managed rules:** SQL injection patterns in request parameters · Cross-site scripting payloads in headers and body · Known exploit payloads from CVE signatures · Volumetric DDoS at Layer 7 (HTTP flood) · Bad bot signatures and scraper patterns.

**The rule to remember:** enable a WAF on any public-facing application from day one — before it receives a single request from the internet. Retroactively adding WAF protection after a breach investigation is a consistently documented finding in post-incident reports. Free tier is available on all three cloud providers.

slides.note: **HashiCorp Vault is powerful but operationally complex.** For projects under 50 engineers, cloud-native secret managers are simpler, cheaper, fully managed, and more than sufficient. Use Vault when you need multi-cloud or on-premises secret management at scale.
slides.time: 20 min · Enable WAF on your cloud load balancer or API gateway

slides.quote: Cloud security architecture roles ($140–200k+) require combining deep technical security knowledge + cloud-native tooling expertise + the communication ability to explain risk in business terms to non-technical stakeholders. The path is: hands-on labs now → certifications → architecture design projects with documented decision rationale → leadership. You are building the foundation every senior architect once built.
slides.sub: The secrets management Python script you build today is a real portfolio artifact. A GitHub repo containing a working Python script that retrieves secrets at runtime via IAM role — with no hardcoded credentials anywhere in the codebase and a clear README explaining the architecture — demonstrates both cloud security knowledge and Python proficiency in a single concrete deliverable. This type of project repo gets mentioned by name in interviews.
slides.mistake: Treating Zero Trust as a product you buy rather than an architecture principle you implement. Zero Trust is not a tool or a checkbox. It is a design philosophy applied to every decision: every network connection requires authentication, every identity gets minimum permissions, every data access gets logged, every workload gets isolated. The controls you have been building all phase — IAM least privilege, private subnets, secret managers, WAF, logging — are all Zero Trust in practice.

cover.title: Container Security: Docker Hardening & __Image Scanning__
cover.hook: 80% of companies run containers in production. Misconfigured Docker = instant root access to the host. Here's how to harden it.

slides.label: Container vs VM Security Model
slides.method: WHAT — Why Containers Are Not Isolation Boundaries
slides.heading: Containers: __Not a Security Boundary Like VMs__

body.text: **The most important container security concept:** containers share the host kernel. A container is NOT a security boundary the way a VM is. A VM runs a completely separate OS kernel — a container escape is impossible without a hypervisor vulnerability. A container escape requires only a kernel vulnerability or a Docker misconfiguration — and there are many.

**A successful container escape gives an attacker complete access to the host operating system** and every other container running on it. This means your entire container workload can be compromised from a single vulnerable container if the others are not properly isolated.

**Four fundamental Docker hardening controls:** run as non-root user (USER directive in Dockerfile — never run as UID 0) · read-only filesystem (--read-only flag removes write access to the container filesystem) · drop all Linux capabilities (--cap-drop ALL removes 38 capabilities, add back only what the app needs) · prevent privilege escalation (--no-new-privileges flag prevents setuid binaries from gaining elevated rights).

slides.time: 20 min · docs.docker.com/engine/security · CIS Docker Benchmark

slides.label: The Two Most Dangerous Docker Flags
slides.method: WHAT — Privileged Containers and Docker Socket
slides.heading: Two Flags That Break Container Security Entirely

body.text:
# --privileged: nearly complete host access
# This single flag gives the container:
# - All 38 Linux capabilities
# - Full access to /dev (all devices)
# - Ability to mount host filesystem
# - Ability to write /etc/cron.d and execute as root on host

docker run --privileged myapp  # NEVER do this in production

# Docker socket exposure — full Docker API inside container:
# /var/run/docker.sock mounted inside container = complete escape
# From inside the container an attacker can:
# - Spawn new privileged containers
# - Read other containers filesystems
# - Achieve complete host access

# Detection — first command a pentester runs after landing in a container:
find / -name docker.sock 2>/dev/null
ls -la /var/run/docker.sock

**In any production Dockerfile or docker-compose.yml, seeing --privileged is an immediate critical finding.** It is almost never necessary for legitimate applications. If an engineer cannot explain exactly why --privileged is required, the correct answer is to remove it and find a scoped alternative using specific capability grants.

slides.time: 20 min · Check every docker-compose.yml you have ever written for these flags

slides.label: Trivy Image Scanning
slides.method: HOW — Scanning Every Image Before Deployment
slides.heading: Trivy: __CVE Scanning in 30 Seconds Per Image__

body.text:
# Install Trivy:
brew install aquasecurity/trivy/trivy  # macOS
apt install trivy                       # Ubuntu

# Scan any Docker image for CVEs:
trivy image nginx:latest
trivy image python:3.11-slim
trivy image node:18-alpine  # alpine images have far fewer CVEs

# Output shows:
# Severity: CRITICAL / HIGH / MEDIUM / LOW / UNKNOWN
# CVE ID: CVE-2024-XXXXX
# Package: libssl (affected)
# Fixed Version: 3.0.14-r0 (update to this)

# Scan a Kubernetes cluster:
trivy k8s --all-namespaces

# Integrate in GitHub Actions — block CRITICAL findings:
# trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL myimage:latest

**Alpine-based images consistently have 80–90% fewer CVEs than Debian or Ubuntu-based images.** When building Docker images, always prefer alpine variants unless a specific dependency requires a glibc-based OS. Fewer packages means fewer vulnerabilities — the most effective vulnerability reduction is removing packages that do not need to be there.

slides.time: 25 min · github.com/aquasecurity/trivy — scan nginx:latest right now

slides.label: Docker Bench for Security
slides.method: HOW — Full CIS Benchmark Audit in 60 Seconds
slides.heading: Docker Bench: __Your CIS Compliance Scorecard__

body.text:
# Run the full CIS Docker Benchmark audit:
docker run --rm --net host --pid host \
  --cap-add audit_control \
  -v /etc:/etc:ro \
  -v /lib/systemd:/lib/systemd:ro \
  -v /usr/bin/containerd:/usr/bin/containerd:ro \
  -v /usr/bin/runc:/usr/bin/runc:ro \
  -v /usr/lib/systemd:/usr/lib/systemd:ro \
  -v /var/lib:/var/lib:ro \
  -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock:ro \
  docker/docker-bench-security

# Output categories:
# [PASS]  - Compliant with CIS benchmark
# [WARN]  - Should be reviewed and fixed
# [INFO]  - Informational finding
# [NOTE]  - Notable but lower priority

# Run BEFORE and AFTER hardening steps
# to measure improvement quantitatively

**Docker Bench covers five areas:** Docker daemon configuration security · container runtime settings · Docker image content security · Docker network configuration · Docker security operations practices. A fresh Docker installation on Ubuntu typically shows 30–40 WARN items. Fixing all of them and documenting each remediation is a real security engineering deliverable.

slides.time: 20 min · Run Docker Bench against your lab environment before and after hardening

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Container escape techniques are among the most impressive demonstrations in cloud penetration testing. Understanding them makes you a sharper defender — you know exactly what attackers enumerate the moment they land in a container. Knowing all three cold will impress in any cloud security interview.
slides.sub: The before-and-after CVE comparison from your Trivy scan is a portfolio artifact worth keeping. 'I scanned nginx:latest and found X critical CVEs, then rebuilt with an Alpine base and hardening controls, reducing the count to Y' is a concrete, quantified security improvement — exactly the kind of work that appears in cloud security engineer job descriptions. Document the numbers and add them to your GitHub portfolio README.
slides.mistake: Practising container escape techniques on shared cloud environments. Container escapes in cloud environments affect hypervisor-level host nodes that are shared across multiple tenants. A container escape on a shared cloud instance is a serious security incident regardless of intent. Always practise in completely isolated local environments using Docker Desktop on your own machine — never on any shared or cloud-hosted environment.

cover.title: Kubernetes Security: RBAC, Network Policies & __kube-bench__
cover.hook: Kubernetes runs half the internet's workloads. Misconfigured K8s gives an attacker your entire cluster. Let's fix that.

slides.label: Kubernetes Security Model
slides.method: WHAT — K8s is Permissive by Default
slides.heading: Kubernetes: __Security is Opt-In at Every Layer__

body.text: **Kubernetes has its own IAM (RBAC), network firewall (Network Policies), secrets management, and pod-level security controls (Security Contexts).** Each layer must be explicitly configured — unlike cloud provider services that have secure defaults, Kubernetes is deliberately permissive out of the box to maximise flexibility.

**Default Kubernetes is insecure in four specific ways:** all pods can communicate with all other pods on any port across all namespaces (zero network isolation) · ServiceAccounts have broader default permissions than they should · secrets are only base64-encoded in etcd — not encrypted — meaning anyone with etcd access reads all cluster secrets in plaintext · the default ServiceAccount token is automatically mounted into every pod.

**The mental model for K8s security:** every default is permissive, every security control is optional, every security decision requires an explicit opt-in. This makes Kubernetes powerful and flexible — and makes misconfigured Kubernetes clusters extremely common in production environments.

slides.time: 20 min · kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/security

slides.label: Kubernetes RBAC
slides.method: WHAT — K8s Identity and Permission System
slides.heading: K8s RBAC: __Audit the Default SA First__

body.text:
# Check what the default ServiceAccount can do:
# (The answer on most default clusters is alarming)
kubectl auth can-i --list \
  --as system:serviceaccount:default:default

# Create a namespace-scoped Role:
kubectl create role pod-reader \
  --verb=get,list,watch \
  --resource=pods \
  --namespace=production

# Bind it to a ServiceAccount:
kubectl create rolebinding pod-reader-binding \
  --role=pod-reader \
  --serviceaccount=production:myapp \
  --namespace=production

# Audit all ClusterRoleBindings (cluster-wide permissions):
kubectl get clusterrolebindings -o wide
# Look for: system:anonymous, default ServiceAccount bindings
# to cluster-admin — immediate critical findings

**Role vs ClusterRole:** a Role is namespace-scoped. A ClusterRole applies across the entire cluster. A ClusterRoleBinding attaches a ClusterRole to a subject cluster-wide. Never grant ClusterRoles when a namespace-scoped Role provides sufficient access — the blast radius of a compromised account with cluster-admin is every resource in every namespace.

slides.time: 20 min · Run the auth can-i audit on your minikube cluster now

slides.label: Network Policies
slides.method: HOW — Adding L3/L4 Firewall Rules to Kubernetes
slides.heading: Network Policies: __Zero Isolation by Default__

body.text:
# Default: ALL pods can reach ALL pods on ALL ports.
# The fix: start with deny-all, then add explicit allows.

# Deny-all ingress for a namespace (apply first):
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector: {}  # applies to ALL pods in namespace
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  # No ingress rules = deny everything

---
# Allow only frontend pods to reach backend on port 8080:
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: production
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: frontend
    ports:
    - port: 8080

**Network Policies require a compatible CNI plugin.** The default Kubernetes networking does not enforce Network Policies even if you create them. Install Calico or Cilium as your CNI — these are the two most widely deployed CNI plugins that fully enforce Network Policies in production clusters.

slides.time: 25 min · Deploy deny-all NetworkPolicy in your minikube namespace

kube-bench/main/job.yaml

cover.title: Cloud Penetration Testing: CloudGoat & __SSRF Attacks__
cover.hook: Cloud pentesting is the highest-paid security specialty. Today you do your first real deliberately-vulnerable cloud attack lab.

slides.label: Cloud Pentest Legal Requirements
slides.method: WHAT — The Legal Framework Before Any Testing
slides.heading: Cloud Pentest: __Written Permission First, Always__

body.text: **Every cloud provider has a penetration testing policy — read it before any engagement.** AWS pre-authorizes testing your own resources for most services without prior notification. GCP and Azure have similar pre-authorization frameworks. The key word is 'your own' — you must own the account or have written permission from the account owner.

**Cloud pentests are governed simultaneously by computer crime law AND the provider's acceptable use policy.** Violating either one creates legal exposure. Even with client permission, testing services outside the pre-authorized list on AWS requires explicit approval in writing from AWS before starting. This is not theoretical — cloud providers actively monitor for attack patterns and have suspended accounts mid-engagement.

**The safe approach for learning:** use deliberately vulnerable cloud environments deployed in your OWN AWS account — CloudGoat, flaws.cloud, flaws2.cloud. You own the account, you have permission, you are attacking infrastructure you deployed yourself. This is the only correct way to practise cloud attack techniques without legal exposure.

slides.time: 20 min · github.com/RhinoSecurityLabs/cloudgoat

slides.label: SSRF to AWS Metadata API
slides.method: WHAT — Server-Side Request Forgery in Cloud Environments
slides.heading: SSRF: __The Attack That Steals Cloud Credentials__

body.text:
# The Instance Metadata Service (IMDS) at 169.254.169.254
# provides the EC2 instance's IAM role credentials.

# Step 1: Discover the role name
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\
iam/security-credentials/
# Returns: MyWebAppRole

# Step 2: Steal the temporary credentials
curl http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/\
iam/security-credentials/MyWebAppRole
# Returns: AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, Token, Expiration

# Step 3: Use stolen credentials from attacker machine
export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=ASIA...
export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
export AWS_SESSION_TOKEN=...
aws s3 ls  # Now operating as the EC2 role

**IMDSv2 mitigates this attack** by requiring a token-based request first — a two-step process that SSRF vulnerabilities typically cannot complete. Enforce IMDSv2 on all EC2 instances by setting HttpTokens=required in the instance metadata options. This single configuration change eliminates the most common cloud credential theft attack vector.

slides.time: 25 min · Enforce IMDSv2 on every EC2 instance in your account

slides.label: CloudGoat Lab
slides.method: WHAT — Deliberately Vulnerable AWS in Your Own Account
slides.heading: CloudGoat: __Real Attack Paths in a Safe Lab__

body.text: **CloudGoat** is created by Rhino Security Labs — the team that documented most known AWS attack paths. It uses Terraform to deploy intentionally vulnerable AWS environments inside your own account. Completely legal to attack because you own the infrastructure and you deployed it yourself.

# CloudGoat setup:
pip install cloudgoat
cloudgoat config profile default

# Deploy the IAM privilege escalation scenario:
cloudgoat create iam_privesc_by_rollback
# Outputs: starting credentials with limited permissions

# The attack path:
# 1. Enumerate available IAM permissions
aws iam list-attached-user-policies --user-name target
aws iam list-policy-versions --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::...

# 2. Discover a policy version rollback path
# 3. Roll back to the admin version of the policy
aws iam set-default-policy-version \
  --policy-arn arn:aws:iam::... \
  --version-id v1
# Result: you now have AdministratorAccess

# ALWAYS clean up immediately:
cloudgoat destroy iam_privesc_by_rollback

**Rhino Security Labs documented 21 distinct AWS IAM privilege escalation paths.** The most common in real engagements: iam:CreatePolicyVersion (create a new admin policy version) · iam:AttachUserPolicy (attach AdministratorAccess to yourself) · sts:AssumeRole on a high-privilege role. Tool to automate detection: Pacu.

slides.time: 30 min · Deploy and complete iam_privesc_by_rollback in your own account

slides.label: Pacu — AWS Attack Framework
slides.method: HOW — Automated AWS Enumeration and Exploitation
slides.heading: Pacu: __Metasploit for AWS__

body.text:
# Install Pacu:
pip install pacu
pacu

# Set compromised credentials:
set_keys
# Enter: AccessKeyId, SecretAccessKey, SessionToken

# Full IAM enumeration:
run iam__enum_users_roles_policies_groups

# Automated privilege escalation scan:
run iam__privesc_scan
# Outputs: which escalation paths are available with these creds

# S3 bucket discovery:
run s3__bucket_finder
# Finds all S3 buckets accessible with these credentials

# List all running EC2 across all regions:
run ec2__enum

**Pacu automates what manual cloud enumeration takes hours to accomplish.** In a real cloud penetration test, the first step after obtaining initial credentials is enumeration — what can these credentials access? What permissions do they have? What escalation paths are available? Pacu answers all three questions automatically and logs every action taken for the pentest report.

slides.note: **ONLY use Pacu against accounts you own or have explicit written permission to test.** The tool generates API calls that appear in CloudTrail — testing without permission is immediately detectable and legally actionable.

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Cloud penetration testing commands a 30–50% salary premium over traditional pentesting. The intersection of deep cloud architecture knowledge with offensive security skills is genuinely rare in the job market. Focus on one cloud provider's attack paths entirely before diversifying — AWS has the richest free lab ecosystem (CloudGoat, flaws.cloud, flaws2.cloud) and makes the ideal starting point.
slides.sub: Checking CloudTrail logs after completing a CloudGoat scenario is one of the highest-value learning activities of this entire phase. You just performed a real IAM privilege escalation — now look at exactly how it appears in the audit logs. Which API calls fired? What did the `userIdentity` field show? What sequence of events would alert a SOC analyst? This dual attacker-and-defender perspective — seeing the attack from both sides simultaneously — is what makes genuinely effective cloud security engineers.
slides.mistake: Forgetting to run terraform destroy after CloudGoat scenarios. A single forgotten NAT Gateway and EC2 instance left running generates $50+ in AWS charges within 24 hours. CloudGoat deploys real, billable AWS resources. After every scenario, run `cloudgoat destroy SCENARIO_NAME` and then verify in the AWS console that no EC2 instances, NAT Gateways, or RDS instances remain running in any region. Check AWS Cost Explorer the next day to confirm zero charges accumulated.

cover.title: Cloud Compliance: CIS Benchmarks & __IaC Security__
cover.hook: Every enterprise cloud deployment must prove compliance. Here are the frameworks every cloud security professional must know cold.

slides.label: CIS Benchmarks for Cloud
slides.method: WHAT — The Compliance Baseline Every Enterprise Uses
slides.heading: CIS Benchmarks: __The Universal Cloud Hardening Standard__

body.text: **The Center for Internet Security** publishes free hardening guides for AWS, Azure, and GCP — the CIS Cloud Benchmarks. These are the most widely referenced cloud security standards in enterprise environments. Any cloud security role at a company handling sensitive data will reference CIS Benchmark controls.

**CIS AWS Foundations Benchmark v1.4** has four control levels — Level 1 is the required minimum baseline for any production account. It covers IAM configuration, logging requirements, monitoring alerts, networking settings, and S3 security. AWS Security Hub, GCP Security Command Center, and Azure Defender for Cloud all have built-in automated CIS checks — your compliance score is visible in the console right now.

**NIST Cybersecurity Framework five functions:** Identify (asset inventory, risk assessment) · Protect (access control, data security) · Detect (anomaly detection, SIEM) · Respond (incident response planning) · Recover (recovery planning, improvements). Every security control built in this 90-day series maps directly to one of these five functions.

slides.time: 20 min · cisecurity.org/benchmark — download the free PDFs

slides.label: Infrastructure as Code Security
slides.heading: IaC Security: __Find It in Code, Not in a Breach__

body.text: **Infrastructure as Code** (Terraform, CloudFormation, Ansible) defines your cloud environment as version-controlled code. The security implication: misconfigurations in IaC files get deployed to production automatically — unless they are caught by scanning first.

# Install Checkov — free IaC security scanner:
pip install checkov

# Scan a directory of Terraform files:
checkov -d ./terraform/

# Scan a specific file:
checkov -f main.tf

# Example findings Checkov catches:
# CKV_AWS_20: S3 bucket has public read ACL
# CKV_AWS_23: Security group allows all ports to 0.0.0.0/0
# CKV_AWS_16: RDS instance is not encrypted
# CKV_AWS_86: CloudTrail is not enabled
# CKV_AWS_119: DynamoDB not encrypted with KMS CMK

# Fail build on any HIGH or CRITICAL finding:
checkov -d . --check HIGH,CRITICAL --hard-fail-on CRITICAL

**Shift left means catching security issues earlier in the development process.** The cost of fixing a misconfiguration in code review is hours. The cost of fixing it after a breach investigation is measured in millions. Checkov in CI/CD makes this enforcement automatic and frictionless.

slides.time: 25 min · pip install checkov — scan your existing Terraform files

slides.label: Checkov in CI/CD
slides.method: HOW — Automating Compliance Before Every Deployment
slides.heading: Checkov in GitHub Actions: __Block Insecure Merges__

body.text:
# .github/workflows/security-scan.yml
name: IaC Security Scan

on:
  push:
    branches: [main]
  pull_request:
    branches: [main]

jobs:
  checkov-scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - name: Checkout code
        uses: actions/checkout@v3

      - name: Run Checkov IaC scan
        uses: bridgecrewio/checkov-action@master
        with:
          directory: terraform/
          framework: terraform
          soft_fail: false       # Block PR on CRITICAL findings
          output_format: sarif   # Integrate with GitHub Security tab

# Result: every PR with insecure Terraform is blocked
# automatically before it can be merged to main

**This is DevSecOps in practice.** Security review happens automatically at the code level before any deployment. The engineer gets immediate feedback in the PR — not a security alert three weeks later after a cloud breach. This workflow is the standard at security-mature engineering organisations.

slides.time: 20 min · Add Checkov to your GitHub Actions workflow

slides.label: SOC 2 in Cloud Environments
slides.method: WHAT — Cloud Controls That Satisfy SOC 2
slides.heading: SOC 2: __The Compliance Framework Enterprise Sales Demands__

body.text: **SOC 2 Type II** is a Service Organization Controls audit evaluating security, availability, processing integrity, confidentiality, and privacy over a 6–12 month observation period. Enterprise SaaS companies require SOC 2 to win contracts with enterprise customers — it is a prerequisite for the sales process at most large organisations.

**Cloud-native controls that directly satisfy SOC 2 requirements:** CloudTrail/Cloud Logging for audit trail (CC6.2, CC7.2) · encryption at rest and in transit (CC6.7) · MFA enforcement policies (CC6.1) · Git-versioned change management (CC6.8) · incident response runbooks (CC7.3) · quarterly access reviews (CC6.3).

**Compliance is the floor of security, never the ceiling.** A system can be fully SOC 2 compliant and breached via a zero-day or social engineering the same week. Use compliance frameworks as a starting point for establishing baselines — not as a definition of 'secure'. The mindset is: compliance tells you the minimum. Security engineering tells you what's actually needed.

slides.quote: Compliance knowledge is a significant salary differentiator for cloud security roles at enterprise companies and regulated industries — finance, healthcare, government, and insurance all pay compliance premiums of 15–25%. Being able to say 'I understand CIS Benchmark controls, map them to our cloud environment, and use Checkov in our CI/CD pipeline to enforce them automatically before deployment' is a concrete, verifiable capability that commands higher compensation.

All checks pass.' — with the actual Terraform code — demonstrates DevSecOps competence in a concrete, verifiable way. This type of contribution to a portfolio repo, linked from LinkedIn, consistently generates recruiter outreach from cloud security teams.
slides.mistake: Treating compliance as security. A CIS Benchmark Level 1 compliant account can still be breached through social engineering, zero-day vulnerabilities, insider threats, and supply chain attacks — none of which CIS Benchmark checks can detect or prevent. Use CIS as a configuration baseline and compliance starting point. Layer in GuardDuty, Security Hub, CloudTrail analysis, and incident response capability on top. Compliance tells you the minimum acceptable configuration. Security engineering tells you what's actually needed to detect and respond to real attacks.

cover.title: Cloud Security Automation with __Python & boto3__
cover.hook: Manual cloud security checks don't scale. Python + cloud APIs = automated defense running 24/7 without you.

slides.label: boto3 — AWS SDK for Python
slides.method: WHAT — Python Access to the Entire AWS API
slides.heading: boto3: __Every AWS Console Action, Now in Python__

body.text: **boto3** is the official AWS Python SDK. Every action available in the AWS console — creating resources, modifying configurations, querying data, sending notifications — has an equivalent boto3 call you can automate. The Python skills from Phase 4 of this series apply directly here.

# Install and authenticate:
pip install boto3

# Authentication options (use IAM Role in production):
# 1. IAM Role on EC2/Lambda — credentials automatic, zero config
# 2. Environment variables:
#    export AWS_ACCESS_KEY_ID=...
#    export AWS_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=...
# 3. ~/.aws/credentials file: aws configure

import boto3

# Access different AWS services:
ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')         # EC2 API
s3 = boto3.resource('s3')          # S3 object-level API
iam = boto3.client('iam')          # IAM API
cloudtrail = boto3.client('cloudtrail')  # CloudTrail API

# Test your connection:
print(boto3.client('sts').get_caller_identity())

**The distinction between boto3.client and boto3.resource:** client provides lower-level API access returning raw JSON responses. resource provides a higher-level, object-oriented interface for supported services (S3, EC2, IAM, DynamoDB). Use resource for S3 operations — it is significantly cleaner to read and write.

slides.time: 20 min · boto3.amazonaws.com/v1/documentation/api/latest/index.html

slides.label: S3 Security Auditor
slides.method: HOW — Writing Your First Security Automation Script
slides.heading: S3 Auditor: __30 Lines That Check Every Bucket__

body.text:
import boto3
import csv

def audit_s3_security():
    s3 = boto3.client('s3')
    buckets = s3.list_buckets()['Buckets']
    findings = []

    for bucket in buckets:
        name = bucket['Name']
        finding = {'bucket': name}

        # Check all 4 public access block settings:
        try:
            pab = s3.get_public_access_block(Bucket=name)
            config = pab['PublicAccessBlockConfiguration']
            finding['block_public_acls'] = config.get('BlockPublicAcls')
            finding['block_public_policy'] = config.get('BlockPublicPolicy')
        except:
            finding['block_public_acls'] = 'NOT SET'

        # Check encryption:
        try:
            enc = s3.get_bucket_encryption(Bucket=name)
            finding['encryption'] = enc['ServerSideEncryptionConfiguration']\
                ['Rules'][0]['ApplyServerSideEncryptionByDefault']['SSEAlgorithm']
        except:
            finding['encryption'] = 'NONE'

        # Check versioning:
        ver = s3.get_bucket_versioning(Bucket=name)
        finding['versioning'] = ver.get('Status', 'Disabled')

        findings.append(finding)
        print(f"{'❌' if finding['encryption']=='NONE' else '✅'} {name}")

    return findings

audit_s3_security()

slides.time: 30 min · Build and run this script against your account right now

slides.label: Lambda + EventBridge Auto-Remediation
slides.method: HOW — Auto-Reverting Misconfigurations in Real Time
slides.heading: Auto-Remediation: __Undo Misconfigurations in Seconds__

body.text: **The most powerful cloud security automation pattern:** CloudTrail captures a dangerous API call → EventBridge rule triggers immediately → Lambda function checks if the action violates policy → if yes, automatically reverts the change AND sends a Slack alert to the security team.

# Lambda function — auto-revert open security group:
import boto3
import json

def lambda_handler(event, context):
    ec2 = boto3.client('ec2')
    detail = event['detail']

    # Extract the security group change details
    sg_id = detail['requestParameters']['groupId']
    ip_permissions = detail['requestParameters']['ipPermissions']

    # Check if any rule allows 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports
    for perm in ip_permissions.get('items', []):
        for cidr in perm.get('ipRanges', {}).get('items', []):
            if cidr.get('cidrIp') == '0.0.0.0/0':
                # Auto-revert: remove the offending rule
                ec2.revoke_security_group_ingress(
                    GroupId=sg_id,
                    IpPermissions=[perm]
                )
                print(f'Auto-reverted open rule on {sg_id}')

**This is how enterprise cloud security teams enforce policy at scale** — not by manually checking consoles, but by automated responses that trigger faster than a human analyst can even read the alert. The entire pattern is serverless, requires no infrastructure maintenance, and costs effectively nothing on the AWS free tier.

slides.time: 20 min · Set up the EventBridge → Lambda auto-remediation pattern

slides.label: IAM Credential Report Automation
slides.heading: IAM Audit Automation: __40 Lines, Real Risk Reduction__

body.text:
import boto3
import base64
import csv
import io

def audit_iam_credentials():
    iam = boto3.client('iam')

    # Generate and retrieve credential report:
    iam.generate_credential_report()
    response = iam.get_credential_report()
    csv_content = base64.b64decode(
        response['Content']).decode('utf-8')

    reader = csv.DictReader(io.StringIO(csv_content))
    critical_findings = []

    for user in reader:
        # Flag users with console access and no MFA:
        if (user['password_enabled'] == 'true'
                and user['mfa_active'] == 'false'):
            critical_findings.append({
                'user': user['user'],
                'issue': 'Console access with NO MFA'
            })

        # Flag stale access keys (unused 90+ days):
        for key_num in ['1', '2']:
            last_used = user.get(f'access_key_{key_num}_last_used_date')
            if last_used and last_used != 'N/A':
                # Add date comparison logic here
                pass

    return critical_findings

findings = audit_iam_credentials()
for f in findings:
    print(f"CRITICAL: {f['user']} — {f['issue']}")
