slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: Python + cloud SDK automation is what separates a $70k cloud security analyst from a $120k cloud security engineer. The ability to write tools that continuously enforce security policy at scale — rather than manually clicking through consoles — is a categorically different skill level. The S3 auditor built today can be deployed as an AWS Lambda on a daily schedule for $0, running automated compliance checks every 24 hours forever.
slides.sub: The S3 auditor script in your GitHub repo is a real portfolio artifact that demonstrates both Python proficiency and cloud security knowledge simultaneously in a single concrete deliverable. When applying for cloud security roles, this is the kind of project to reference specifically: 'I built a Python boto3 script that audits every S3 bucket in an AWS account for public access configuration, encryption status, and versioning — runs as a scheduled Lambda at zero cost.' That one sentence describes a real security engineering project, not a certification.
slides.mistake: Granting Lambda functions AdministratorAccess for simplicity. A Lambda with admin permissions that has a code vulnerability or injection flaw becomes an attack tool against your own AWS account. An attacker who exploits the Lambda can use its permissions to do anything in your account. Always grant Lambda execution roles the minimum permissions needed for the specific task — a Lambda that only needs to list S3 buckets and call GetPublicAccessBlock needs exactly those two permissions and nothing else.

cover.title: Cloud Security Certifications & __Career Paths__
cover.hook: Cloud security certs are the highest-ROI certifications in all of cybersecurity right now. Here's the exact path to take.

slides.label: AWS Certification Path
slides.heading: AWS Certs: __Three Steps to Security Specialist__

body.text: **AWS Cloud Practitioner** ($100, approximately 2 weeks study) — cloud fundamentals, shared responsibility, service overview. The foundation for non-technical stakeholders and a credibility baseline for technical candidates who want to prove they know AWS beyond hands-on usage. Free study via AWS Skill Builder.

**AWS Solutions Architect Associate** ($300) — the most popular AWS certification globally. Covers architecture fundamentals, service selection, cost optimisation, and security at the design level. Recommended before the Security Specialty — the architecture knowledge makes security decisions significantly more intuitive.

**AWS Security Specialty SCS-C02** ($300) — the highest-value cloud security certification for AWS-focused roles. Tests: IAM and access control, infrastructure security, data protection, incident response, and threat detection. Average salary for holders: $120k+. Free study: AWS Skill Builder + freeCodeCamp YouTube courses for all three levels.

slides.time: 20 min · aws.amazon.com/certification

slides.label: Azure Certification Path
slides.method: WHAT — Microsoft Cloud Security Certification Roadmap
slides.heading: Azure Certs: __SC-200 is the Hottest Cert Right Now__

body.text: **AZ-900 Azure Fundamentals** ($165) — cloud concepts, Azure services overview, pricing and SLA. The fastest credential to baseline Azure knowledge. Free complete study path on Microsoft Learn with no lab subscription needed.

**AZ-500 Azure Security Engineer Associate** — hands-on security engineering exam covering Entra ID, network security, data and application security, and security operations. Recommended for practitioners who want technical depth before the SC-200.

**SC-200 Security Operations Analyst** — Sentinel and Defender XDR focused. The most in-demand Microsoft security certification right now. KQL is the key skill gap most candidates have. Salary range: $80–120k for roles requiring this certification.

**Every Azure certification has a completely free study path with sandbox labs on learn.microsoft.com.** No Azure subscription needed. The free sandbox environments provide real Azure access for the duration of each learning module.

slides.time: 15 min · learn.microsoft.com — check the SC-200 learning path

slides.label: GCP and Vendor-Neutral Paths
slides.method: WHAT — GCP and Premium Vendor-Neutral Options
slides.heading: GCP + CCSP: __Premium Certs for Senior Roles__

body.text: **GCP Certification path:** Google Cloud Digital Leader (business-focused foundations) → Associate Cloud Engineer (practical hands-on engineering) → **Professional Cloud Security Engineer** (architecture-level, most valued GCP security cert, commands $130k+ average). Google Cloud Skills Boost provides free guided learning paths with monthly lab credits.

**CCSP (Certified Cloud Security Professional)** by ISC2 — the premium vendor-neutral cloud security certification. $599 exam, requires 5 years of work experience (with ISC2 associate path available for those without experience). Widely required for senior cloud security architect roles at enterprise companies and government contractors. The gold standard certification for cloud security leadership roles.

**CCSK (Certificate of Cloud Security Knowledge)** by CSA — $395, the most accessible advanced cloud certification for candidates building toward CCSP. Vendor-neutral, covers cloud architecture, governance, compliance, and operations. A strong addition to any cloud security resume alongside a platform-specific certification.

slides.time: 20 min · cloudskillsboost.google — review Professional Cloud Security Engineer requirements

slides.label: Cloud Security Career Roles
slides.method: WHAT — Five Cloud Security Career Paths and Salaries
slides.heading: Cloud Security Roles: __Five Paths, All Actively Hiring__

body.text: **Cloud Security Engineer ($110–150k)** — builds and maintains security controls in cloud environments. Day-to-day: implementing IAM policies, security group rules, logging configurations, automated compliance checks. Entry into this role typically requires one cloud certification and hands-on lab experience.

**Cloud Penetration Tester ($100–145k)** — offensive testing of cloud environments. Requires deep knowledge of cloud architecture combined with attack technique knowledge. CloudGoat and flaws.cloud experience is directly relevant. Commands a 30–50% salary premium over traditional pentesting.

**Cloud Security Architect ($140–200k)** — designs security at organisational scale. Requires 5+ years of combined cloud and security experience, communication skills for executive stakeholders, and the ability to make security design decisions that scale across hundreds of teams. The long-term target role.

**DevSecOps Engineer ($120–160k)** — integrates security into CI/CD pipelines. Checkov in GitHub Actions, container scanning in build pipelines, secrets management for deployment pipelines. The intersection of development workflow knowledge and security engineering.

steps: **Research** your target certification: exam guide → domain breakdown → question count → passing score → recommended experience
**Build** a 30-day study plan: Day 1–7 (Domain 1), Day 8–14 (Domain 2), Day 15–21 (Domain 3), Day 22–28 (practice exams), Day 29–30 (review gaps)
**Post** the study plan on LinkedIn — tag the certification and use relevant hashtags — this creates accountability

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: The highest-ROI certification decision based on your target environment: working at a Microsoft-heavy enterprise → SC-200 first. Targeting AWS-focused startups or wanting the richest lab ecosystem → AWS Security Specialty. Wanting maximum long-term salary ceiling in large enterprises → CCSP after 2+ years of hands-on cloud experience. Choose based on your specific target environment, not just certification popularity rankings.
slides.sub: Cloud certifications expire. AWS and Azure require renewal every 3 years. GCP every 2 years. Factor renewal exam costs and continuing education into your long-term certification strategy from the beginning. The best approach: set a calendar reminder 6 months before expiry, use that window to catch up on new service features that were added since you originally certified, then renew. Treating renewal as ongoing professional development — rather than an annoying administrative task — keeps your knowledge current and your credential valid.
slides.mistake: Choosing a certification based on which one is cheapest or fastest to pass rather than which one aligns with your specific target job market. The AWS Cloud Practitioner is the easiest AWS certification — but it does not qualify you for security engineer roles. The SC-200 requires real KQL proficiency — but it opens doors that AZ-900 does not. Invest study time in the certification that matches the role you actually want, not the one that looks most achievable right now.

body.text: **Day 71:** Shared responsibility model — IaaS/PaaS/SaaS security boundaries, top cloud attack vectors, CSPM tools (Prowler, Scout Suite), cloud platform selection.

**Day 76:** Azure Entra ID, Conditional Access, PIM, Defender for Cloud Secure Score, Microsoft Sentinel, KQL queries for security analysis.
**Day 77:** Zero Trust principles (NIST SP 800-207), cloud secrets management, WAF deployment, defence in depth seven-layer architecture.
**Day 78:** Docker container security model, privileged flag and docker.sock risks, Trivy image scanning, Docker Bench CIS audit, image hardening.
**Day 79:** Kubernetes RBAC, network policies for pod isolation, kube-bench CIS audit, six common K8s attack paths, minikube lab.
**Day 80:** Cloud pentest legal requirements, SSRF to IMDS attack, CloudGoat deliberately vulnerable AWS, IAM privilege escalation paths, Pacu framework.
**Day 81:** CIS Benchmarks, NIST CSF, SOC 2 cloud controls, Checkov IaC scanning, GitHub Actions DevSecOps pipeline.

slides.label: Portfolio Project — Secure Cloud Architecture
slides.method: HOW — Building Your Cloud Security Portfolio on GitHub
slides.heading: Portfolio Project: __The GitHub Repo That Wins Interviews__

body.text: **Build and document a securely architected cloud environment on GitHub.** This single repository — with working Terraform, a professional README, an architecture diagram, and documented security controls — demonstrates more practical capability to a cloud security hiring manager than most training certificates.

**Required components:** VPC with public and private subnets · EC2 in private subnet with no public IP · S3 with encryption and versioning and all public access blocked · IAM role for EC2 with minimum required permissions · CloudTrail enabled with S3 logging and integrity validation · Security group with minimal open ports · All infrastructure defined as Terraform code passing a full Checkov scan with zero CRITICAL findings.

**The Security Controls section of your README must:** list every implemented control, map it to the specific threat it mitigates, and cite the relevant CIS Benchmark control number. This section demonstrates that you understand why each control exists — not just how to configure it.

slides.time: 90+ min · This is today's primary deliverable — allocate most of your session here

slides.label: Architecture Diagram Requirement
slides.method: HOW — Creating Professional Cloud Architecture Diagrams
slides.heading: Architecture Diagram: __Draw It Before You Present It__

body.text: **Every cloud security portfolio project needs a visual architecture diagram.** Free tools with cloud icon libraries built in: draw.io (cloud-native, supports official AWS/GCP/Azure icon sets, exports to PNG/SVG) · Excalidraw (clean hand-drawn style, excellent for whiteboard-style architecture presentations) · Lucidchart free tier.

**Your diagram must show:** VPC boundary and subnet divisions with CIDR labels · security group boundaries drawn around the EC2 instances they protect · data flow direction arrows showing where traffic can and cannot go · encryption indicators (lock icon) on S3 and any encrypted data stores · NAT Gateway positioned on the public subnet with arrow showing outbound-only direction from private.

**Embed the diagram directly in your GitHub README** using a PNG export. GitHub renders images inline in README files. The diagram should be visible immediately when anyone opens your repository without clicking into any subdirectory. Engineers who can draw infrastructure from memory in interviews consistently outperform those who only know console clicks.

slides.time: 30 min · draw.io — build your VPC architecture diagram before writing any Terraform

slides.label: LinkedIn Update for Phase 8
slides.method: HOW — Documenting Your Cloud Security Achievement
slides.heading: LinkedIn Update: __Make Your Portfolio Discoverable__

body.text: **Add 'Cloud Security' to your LinkedIn Skills section** and endorse the specific tools you used this phase: AWS IAM, Amazon GuardDuty, AWS CloudTrail, Docker Security, Kubernetes Security, Python boto3, Terraform, Checkov, Microsoft Sentinel, KQL.

**Write a LinkedIn post with your architecture diagram** — include a 3-paragraph write-up: what you built, which specific security controls you implemented and why, and one concrete lesson from the phase. Tag: #CloudSecurity #AWS #DevSecOps #Cybersecurity #InfrastructureAsCode. Cloud security hiring managers at top firms actively search and monitor these exact tags.

**The resume bullet point to add today:** 'Built and documented a securely architected cloud environment using AWS — implemented IAM least privilege, VPC network segmentation, CloudTrail audit logging, GuardDuty threat detection, and IaC security scanning with Checkov in a CI/CD pipeline. All infrastructure defined in Terraform with zero CRITICAL security findings.'

slides.note: **Run `terraform destroy`** on all cloud lab resources before starting Phase 9. Do a complete billing review across all three cloud providers you used this phase.

slides.label: Pro Tip
slides.quote: A GitHub repo with working Terraform deploying a securely architected cloud environment — all-pass Checkov scan results, a professional README, a clear architecture diagram, and a documented security controls section — demonstrates more practical capability to a cloud security hiring manager than most training certificates. It proves you can build and secure cloud infrastructure, not just study about it.
slides.sub: Phase 9 — Ethical Hacking — is where everything converges. Networking from Phase 1 tells you what traffic looks like. Linux from Phase 3 tells you how systems work internally. Python from Phase 4 gives you automation for custom tools. Traffic analysis from Phase 5 shows you what attacks look like on the wire. Cloud knowledge from Phase 8 gives you the defensive context. You understand what defenders see. Now you learn how attackers think and move. Everything you built matters for what comes next.
slides.mistake: Leaving cloud lab resources running when Phase 9 starts. Check every cloud account you used this phase: AWS Cost Explorer, GCP Billing dashboard, Azure Cost Management. Delete every EC2 instance, NAT Gateway, RDS instance, and any other paid service. Unused cloud resources accumulate charges silently and indefinitely — they do not stop billing when you stop using them. Run `terraform destroy` on every CloudGoat deployment and any other Terraform-managed infrastructure before starting Day 85.
